explanation.text: Wiesner says his artistic older siblings fascinated him when he watched them draw, and their supplies captivated him. This shows that observing family members first sparked his interest in art.

Both paragraphs linger on the colors, textures, smells, tools, and unusual supplies that Wiesner loved. The statement that art materials were deeply appealing therefore best captures the shared main idea.

Wiesner says he actually tried shooting colored paint with friends, then qualifies the memory with “it sounded like a good idea.” This wording suggests that the experiment did not work as well as he imagined.
Wiesner never describes his parents resisting his decision; instead, he says they were excited about his choice. This shows that they let him pursue art and were glad when he chose art school.

Before art school, Wiesner’s creativity was private and he expected pressure to choose a different career. Finding a place full of other artists revealed many possibilities, which explains the image of doors opening wide.

As a child, Wiesner stood out as “the kid who could draw,” unlike most classmates. At art school everyone shared that identity, which shows that he finally found a community of similarly creative people.

The passage traces Wiesner’s constant drawing from early childhood through his eventual choice of art school and an artistic career. Therefore, his lifelong interest in drawing and painting is essential to a summary.

Yasmeen “dragged” her backpack while worrying about being the only Indian student and what classmates would think. This slow, heavy action shows that she is unhappy rather than simply carrying many books.

Yasmeen usually feels excited when relatives gather for Eid-ul-Fitr, but this year she shrugs and worries about what to present. This contrast shows that the school assignment is troubling her during the celebration.
Yasmeen handles the worn, leather-bound Koran carefully and knows her father would never allow her to take it to school. These details show that the book is precious to him.

Yasmeen is still searching for a custom when she watches Amma prepare henna paste and fill the cones. This scene hints at the solution because mehndi made from that paste becomes Yasmeen’s presentation.

Paragraph 12 describes Amma’s ornate salwar kameez for the party, and paragraph 19 shows all the women and girls gathered in glittering dresses. Together these details show that fine clothing is part of the celebration.
Yasmeen expects uncertainty about her tradition, but Mrs. Cross asks for a mehndi design and classmates eagerly join in. Her gasp therefore reveals surprise at their enthusiastic response.

Yasmeen initially fears classmates will laugh at her customs, yet they respond eagerly to her mehndi presentation and she rushes home happily. This outcome shows that sharing a family tradition can be rewarding.

These paragraphs establish the field and grove where Tom is writing, then introduce Tom and the black fox as they approach one another. This shows how the section sets the scene and presents the main characters.
Tom’s paper shakes, his heart seems caught in his throat, and he says he has never been so excited. These physical reactions show that seeing the fox gives him an intense thrill.
At first the fox moves toward Tom because she has not seen him. In paragraphs 6 and 7, the wind carries his scent and she looks directly at him, which shows that she has become aware of Tom.

At zoos, Tom hurried past foxes displayed before artificial scenery and never truly observed them. The field experience is different because he watches a rare black fox move naturally and freely in the wild.
Tom thinks the sentence “Today I saw a black fox” makes the most exciting event of his life sound like nothing. He hesitates because he cannot find words powerful enough to capture the experience.

The huge word “WAR!” on the old newspaper leaves Tom unable to speak, and he imagines “FOX!” in the same bold form after his encounter. This comparison shows that both words inspire an overwhelming response in him.
Tom remains still after the fox leaves, cannot believe what happened, and finds ordinary words unable to express it. These reactions show that the rare encounter moves him deeply.

Paragraph 4 warns that constant texting can replace face-to-face time and hurt family relationships. Kenny’s mother helping him for the first time in two years supports that claim because phone-free time renewed their interaction.
Dr. Dowdell describes an expectation of being reachable every moment and explains that this leaves no downtime. This shows that constant access and nonstop texting have become habits for many teens.

The results section alternates between Kenny’s reduced stress, sleep, schoolwork, and family time and Franchesca’s exercise, friends, and better sleep. This organization is a comparison because it shows each teen’s effects side by side.

Both teens begin uncertain about losing their phones but discover better sleep, exercise, schoolwork, relationships, or reduced stress. These unexpected benefits show that teens may not realize how different phone-free life can be.

Kenny calls the challenge torture and does not plan to repeat it, despite recognizing benefits. Franchesca says she loved the experience and continues it, which shows that only she fully appreciates the phone-free freedom.

The article explains concerns about texting’s effects on sleep, school, health, and relationships, then tests those concerns with two teens. Therefore, experts’ worry about how texting affects teenagers best expresses the main idea.

Without constant texting, the teens use their time for sleep, exercise, schoolwork, family, and in-person friends. This broader change is important to a summary because it captures the experiment’s main result.
